provider.name: Coastal Elements AI (with Poly186.io delivery support)
provider.role: AI and automation consulting / implementation provider

contacts.name: Dylan Ryan
contacts.notes: Primary business and account lead; based in Virginia Beach; focuses on AI strategy for SMBs and mid-market.

contacts.name: Princeps Polycap
contacts.notes: AI/LLM and automation technical architect; focuses on Azure/OpenAI stack and model selection.

contacts.name: Drew (last name not stated)
contacts.notes: Coordinates teams, manages timelines and resources, client communication, and delivery.

contacts.name: Richard
contacts.role: Broker or internal stakeholder at S.L. Nusbaum
contacts.notes: Made the initial connection between Dylan/Coastal Elements and S.L. Nusbaum; appears to be in brokerage group.

client.name: S.L. Nusbaum Realty Co. (referred to as SL Nesbaum / Nesbom / SL Nusbaum)
client.role: Client – commercial and multifamily real estate owner/manager

contacts.name: Mark Ashley
contacts.role: IT Director (previously consultant and interim IT director)
contacts.notes: Primary client contact; responsible for IT strategy, Azure/365 environment, AI governance, and budget.

contacts.name: Executive Committee (C‑suite, not individually named)
contacts.notes: Require clear ROI and marketable client-facing AI offerings to approve larger investments.

contacts.name: Department Heads (HR, Finance/Accounting, Multifamily operations, Brokerage, etc.)
contacts.notes: Mark will be gathering their AI use cases and needs via workshops/meetings.

engagementType: Potential future: Productized/SaaS solution design for client-facing offering

deliverables: Initial AI/automation discovery and process audit across key departments (HR, Finance/Accounting, Multifamily operations, Brokerage, etc.).
Written workshop framework and departmental discovery question sets to guide Mark’s internal meetings (with focus on both process efficiency and marketable offerings).
ROI-oriented use case list for S.L. Nusbaum, prioritizing quick wins (e.g., documentation-heavy, manual, and workflow-heavy processes).
Recommended AI/automation solution concepts aligned to S.L. Nusbaum’s Azure / Microsoft 365 environment (e.g., leveraging Azure OpenAI, fine-tuned models, existing vendor features).

Optional: Design and implementation of specific automations/agents (e.g., contract review assistant, document processing, multifamily/utility or property-focused workflows) within S.L. Nusbaum’s Azure tenant.
Optional: Design of a differentiated, client-facing AI-enabled offering S.L. Nusbaum can market (for large brokerage/management deals).

phases.name: Phase 1 – Discovery & Internal Alignment
phases.description: Mark conducts one-on-ones and departmental meetings (executives, department heads, brokers) to gather needs and pain points; Coastal Elements supports with workshop frameworks and question prompts.
activities: Mark’s internal discovery with executives and department heads.
Department-head meeting later in the month (client-side, pre-consulting).
Brokers’ monthly meeting in January where Mark expects more specific AI/custom needs to emerge.
Coastal Elements sends workshop templates and discovery question documents.
dependencies: Completion of budget season and initial 2025 IT draft budget by Mark.
Mark’s internal intel gathering on AI expectations and priorities.

phases.name: Phase 2 – Joint Workshops / AI & Process Audit
phases.description: Coastal Elements runs focused workshops with specific departments to map processes, identify time sinks, and tie use cases to KPIs/OKRs.
activities: Workshops with HR, Finance/Accounting, Multifamily operations, Brokerage, and other key groups.
Process and data audit (what data exists, where it lives, how it’s used; SOPs and workflow mapping).
Identification of high-ROI opportunities (especially documentation-heavy, manual, and fragmented processes).
Assessment of existing vendor-provided AI features to avoid unnecessary custom builds.
outputs: Prioritized use case list with quantified time-savings/ROI estimates.
Recommendations on which use cases suit quick POCs vs. larger initiatives.
Guidance on which vendor AI features to leverage vs. where to build on Azure.

phases.name: Phase 3 – POC / Pilot Implementations
phases.description: Build 1–2 prioritized POCs or pilots to demonstrate concrete value (time savings, accuracy) and help secure executive buy-in.
activities: Design and implementation of small, targeted automation or AI agents (e.g., contract redlining assistant, document ingestion/report generation agent, expanded utility-bill-like workflows).
Deployment primarily within S.L. Nusbaum’s Azure environment (using Azure OpenAI and other Azure services); POC may initially be hosted by provider if needed.
Measurement of baseline vs. post-implementation metrics (time spent, error rates, throughput) against existing KPIs/OKRs.
Documentation for leadership detailing ROI and scalability.
outputs: Working POC/pilot solution accessible to relevant teams.
ROI report (time saved, cost estimates, qualitative benefits).
Decision recommendations on scaling or productizing the solution.

phases.name: Phase 4 – Scaled Solution / Productization (Optional)
phases.description: If desired by S.L. Nusbaum, design and build a larger, marketable AI-enabled solution that can be offered to S.L. Nusbaum’s own clients (owners, investors, etc.).
activities: Define product vision and value proposition (e.g., “AI-enhanced property management intelligence and reporting” for large deals).
Decide deployment model: fully in S.L. Nusbaum’s Azure tenant vs. provider-managed SaaS.
Technical architecture, build, and integration (potentially including fine-tuned models on Nusbaum contracts, property data, utility/billing data, etc.).
Governance, security, and compliance review with Mark (guardrails for AI usage and data exposure).
outputs: Client-facing AI product/feature set S.L. Nusbaum can market in large transactions.
Operational runbooks and documentation, including governance guidelines.

milestones.name: Internal discovery & budget season (client-side)
milestones.details: Mark is in the middle of multifamily and corporate budget season; he has submitted his first draft for next year including some AI-focused consulting hours.

milestones.name: Department head meeting
milestones.targetDate: Later this month (relative to Nov 5 meeting)
milestones.details: Mark expects to get more insight from department heads on AI needs and process pain points.

milestones.name: Brokers’ meeting with Mark
milestones.targetDate: January (monthly brokers meeting; specifically mentioned for January)
milestones.details: Mark expects more ‘one-off’ and custom AI requests, particularly from brokerage group; major source of potential high-value use cases.

milestones.name: Mid-January follow-up with Coastal Elements
milestones.details: Regroup to review what Mark has learned from executives, department heads, and brokers; evaluate concrete opportunities and potential partnership scope.

milestones.name: Pre–January informal meeting
milestones.details: Dylan and Mark to schedule lunch/beers to deepen relationship and discuss ideas informally.

milestones.name: Post–January: potential POCs and workshops
milestones.targetDate: To be determined (likely Q1–Q2 following January follow-up)
milestones.details: Dependent on internal demand and executive committee appetite for ROI-positive and marketable AI initiatives.

commercialTerms.roiExpectation: Executive committee will require clear, quantified ROI beyond internal time savings; they particularly want AI initiatives that can be marketed externally as part of S.L. Nusbaum’s offering in large ($100–300M) deals, in addition to internal process improvements and labor savings.

nextSteps.action: Set a calendar placeholder for a mid-January follow-up meeting with Mark to revisit AI opportunities after his internal discovery.
nextSteps.source: “We will absolutely, I'll set something up for mid-January, it's kind of a fall-up there.”

nextSteps.action: Prepare and email Mark a workshop framework and sample discovery questions targeted at key departments (HR, Finance, Multifamily, Brokerage, etc.), including prompts that surface both process/efficiency use cases and potentially marketable AI products.
nextSteps.source: “I'm going to... put together a couple things just to send over, like, hey, you know, this is an example of, like, what a workshop would look like, a framework there, maybe some, you know, questions that might be good from certain departments...”

nextSteps.action: Conduct one-on-one conversations with executives and department heads to gather their AI-related needs, pain points, and expectations, using the provided workshop materials where helpful.
nextSteps.source: “allowing me some time to, you know, really start having these one-on-ones with, you know, the executives and find out where their brains are at.”

nextSteps.action: Participate in department head meeting later this month and brokers’ meeting in January to collect concrete AI use cases (especially from brokers).
nextSteps.source: “we have a department head meeting later this month... and then some of the brokers, they have a monthly meeting that they invited me to in January.”

nextSteps.action: Schedule and hold an informal lunch/beer meeting before the January follow-up to strengthen relationship and possibly discuss ideas.
nextSteps.source: “would love to grab lunch or beers at some point... I'm genuinely going to reach out, see if can grab lunch or something here, even before we meet next.”

nextSteps.action: At mid-January follow-up, review collected use cases and identify 1–2 high-ROI quick wins suitable for initial POC/pilot, then scope potential engagement.
nextSteps.source: “we'll reconnect beginning of the year and see kind of where you guys are and where the lowest hanging fruit is for us to, you know, explore a partnership and then, you know, kind of go from there.”

keyQuotes: “I really think that, you know, a partnership between our groups, I see it being more of a, you know, more focusing on developing a solution, not so much answering a problem we have… But I think more vision is what a group like yours could offer us.”

“What I'm seeing for… late 26 into 27, I think what you're going to start seeing is companies like ours really starting to market what AI solutions they have and how we're offering AI to benefit our customers. And if we're not doing that, then I think that it's going to pass us by.”

“The big thing for us is you just got to find one quick win for the organization, show them kind of what that is, and then it's a lot easier for them to comprehend and understand… once they see it in their lens, through their lens, in their kind of workflow and their business, everyone's like, okay, I want that for me over here in sales now…”

“We first start to audit… we audit the processes you have, the actual data you have… And then we give you a recommendation… If the client doesn't have any infrastructure in place, then… we'll host it on our side… And once they're ready to actually deploy it onto their side… then we can move into that stage.”
